Rideshare accidents in Dallas are more legally complex than most people realize. The moment an Uber or Lyft driver opens their app, an entirely different insurance structure activates — one with multiple coverage tiers that can directly affect what compensation you receive after a crash.

Under Texas law, injured parties have just 2 years to file a claim. Miss that window, and you forfeit your right to pursue damages entirely. For passengers, other drivers, and pedestrians caught in rideshare collisions, understanding which insurance tier applies — and who is liable — can make or break a case.

If you've been in an Uber or Lyft accident in Dallas, you're probably dealing with more confusion than you expected. Who pays? Is it the driver's insurance? Uber's? Lyft's? The answer actually depends on what the driver was doing the moment the crash happened — and most people have no idea that the rules change depending on whether the app was open, a ride was accepted, or a passenger was in the car.

Texas gives you 2 years to file a claim after a rideshare accident. That sounds like a long time, but evidence disappears fast — app data, driver logs, witness memories. The sooner you understand your rights, the better your chances of getting fair compensation.

reddit_title: Got hit by an Uber driver in Dallas — here's what I learned about how rideshare insurance actually works in Texas
reddit_body:
Rideshare accidents in Texas are tricky because there isn't one simple insurance policy that covers everything. Uber and Lyft use a tiered system: if the driver's app is off, it's their personal insurance. If the app is on but no ride is accepted, there's limited contingent coverage. Once a ride is accepted or a passenger is in the car, the full $1 million commercial policy kicks in. Knowing which tier applies to your crash is the first step.

Texas has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims, which sounds like plenty of time but really isn't. App data, GPS logs, and witness accounts can all disappear quickly. If you were injured as a passenger, another driver, a cyclist, or a pedestrian, you should document everything immediately — photos, screenshots of the trip, and any medical visits.

If you're not sure where to start with finding an attorney who actually handles rideshare cases, wreckmatch.com has a free matching tool that connects you with lawyers from a large network who specialize in exactly this. Worth checking out before you try cold-calling firms.
